chapters.title: The shallow view, and the deep view
Recap — GitHub for models, and where most people stop
The mental shift — a model is not a file
The car analogy — what an open model actually needs
Layer 1 — Provenance: where did this model come from?

segments.text: Welcome to Lesson 1.
Most tutorials teach you to download a model. This lesson teaches you something different.
The shallow view of HuggingFace is: find a model name, paste it into from_pretrained, run.
That works. A lot of careers stop there.
The deep view asks: where did this model come from, what files define it, can I trust it, can I reproduce it?
That's the difference between using HuggingFace and understanding HuggingFace.
By the end of this lesson, HuggingFace should stop looking like a website.
It should start looking like the operating system for open AI.
Quick recap of where we were in the May 8 office hours.
HuggingFace is the GitHub for models. The model card is a README.
Source code is small text. Model weights are gigabytes of binary.
GitHub caps individual files at around 100 megabytes. So HuggingFace built Git LFS on top, and then moved beyond it to Xet — content-addressed storage tuned for the way models are downloaded.
We talked about scale. One older model alone hit eighteen million downloads in a single month.
And we talked about the standard files in a model repo: README, config.json, tokenizer files, and the weights themselves as safetensors, dot bin, or gguf.
If that's where your mental model of HuggingFace stopped — you're still at the surface.
Because what HuggingFace is actually doing is way bigger than file hosting.
Let me show you.
Here's the mental shift this lesson is built around.
A model is not a file. A model is a node in a graph.
Every model has a lineage. A paper. A base checkpoint. A pretraining dataset. A supervised fine-tuning dataset. A preference dataset. Quantizations. Fine-tunes. Demos that use it. Apps built on it.
So when you see Qwen slash Qwen3 0.6B on a model page, that one line is the tip of a tree.
Underneath it are paper releases, dataset cards, evaluation runs, derivative quantizations, hosted spaces, third-party fine-tunes, and discussions.
The model hub looks like a website. Underneath, it's a graph database — models, datasets, spaces, papers, collections, eval results, commits, licences.
A good model page should answer four questions. What is this. Where did it come from. What depends on it. What can I safely do with it.
If a page doesn't answer those — that's a signal about the model itself.
And now we get to the analogy I want you to keep.
A model file is like an engine.
But a car is not an engine. A car needs a chassis. A fuel system. A safety certificate. A user manual. A service history. A dashboard. A road test. Ownership papers. A repair network.
An engine on the floor of a garage is not a car. It is a component.

An open model is the same. The weights are the engine. To turn them into something trustworthy and usable, you need a config, a tokenizer, metadata, a licence, evals, dataset lineage, a safety scan, runtime options, a demo, and community feedback.
Each of those is a layer of the supply chain. Each layer is something you can inspect.
We're going to walk through the six most important layers — provenance, datasets, security, evals, runtime, agents.
By the end you'll have a vocabulary to inspect any open model release in the world.
Let's start with provenance.
Layer one. Provenance. Where did this model come from?
The naive question is: did this come from HuggingFace? That's not provenance. HuggingFace is the host. Provenance is who made it, when, from what.

Real questions: is this the official model? Is this a derivative? What paper introduced it? What dataset was used? Is there an instruct version? Is there a quantized version? What evals exist? Who is maintaining it?
Here's why it matters. Right now if you search any popular model name on HuggingFace, you'll get the official repo — and you'll get dozens of forks, mirrors, and re-uploads from random accounts.
Some are legitimate quantizations from trusted maintainers. Some are people who downloaded the weights, re-uploaded them, and changed nothing. Some are forks with quietly modified code.
The official Qwen team's repo and a random account's repo can have identical model names. They are not the same artifact.
So your first habit: always trace the model back to the organisation that trained it. Check the verified badge. Read the paper link.
And start thinking of any model as a node in a tree — there is an official root, and there are branches. You want to know which one you're looking at.
Same habit, applied to datasets. That's layer two.
Layer two. Datasets. And the rule I want you to write down — bad data silently becomes bad model behaviour.
Most people think a dataset is something you call load_dataset on. That's the beginner view. A dataset is not a file. A dataset is a queryable training surface.
HuggingFace ships a Dataset Viewer on every dataset page. Most learners scroll past it. It is one of the most important pieces of infrastructure on the site.
It tells you what columns exist, how many rows, what splits, lets you preview rows, search inside the text, filter by category, see statistics, estimate token counts, find duplicates.
Before you train a model on data you didn't build, inspect it. Before you evaluate a model on a benchmark you didn't write, inspect the benchmark.

Half the surprises in model behaviour come from dataset quirks that nobody bothered to look at. Toxic rows. Mis-labelled categories. Duplicate prompts inflating eval scores. PII you weren't expecting.
The Dataset Viewer is not UI decoration. It's the difference between flying blind and flying with instruments.
Use it on every dataset. Even when it feels like overkill.
Layer three. Security. And the most important sentence of this lesson.
When you write trust_remote_code equals True in from_pretrained, you are giving a stranger permission to run their Python code on your machine.
Not metaphorically. Literally. The flag downloads modeling_dot_py files from the repo and executes them on import.
Beginners hit a load error, paste trust_remote_code equals True, the load works, and they move on. That's not a workaround. That's a security decision.
Here's the tiering I want you to internalise. Low risk files: config dot json, tokenizer dot json, model dot safetensors, README. These are data, not code.

Higher risk files: pytorch_model dot bin and other pickle formats — pickle can execute arbitrary code on load. Custom modeling underscore py files. Custom tokenization underscore py files. requirements dot txt that pulls who-knows-what. app dot py from a Space.
The professional habit: before you load a model from an unfamiliar repo, read its file list. If you see custom Python or pickle weights, slow down. Read the code. Or pick a different repo.
A model repo is a software supply chain artifact. The same way you wouldn't pip install a package nobody you trust has reviewed, you don't load a model from a stranger without checking what runs.
Most popular models — Qwen, Llama, Mistral, the well-known ones — ship safetensors and need no custom code. That is a feature. That is what you want.
Layer four. Evals and reproducibility. The shortest version of this layer is: a leaderboard without reproducibility is just marketing.
Open any model card. You will see phrases like strong reasoning, better coding, improved instruction following, fast inference, small but powerful.

The questions that turn marketing into evidence: compared to what? On which benchmark? With which prompt format? With which decoding settings? On which hardware? Can I reproduce it? Where are the raw outputs?

A serious evaluation isn't a single number. It's a pipeline: benchmark dataset, prompt template, model runner, raw generations saved, scoring script, metrics, result file, leaderboard, and reproducibility notes.
If a model claims a score and you can't find the prompts used, the decoding parameters, or the raw outputs — treat the claim as advertising, not data.
And when you ship your own models later in this cohort, this is the standard you'll be held to. Not just score equals zero point seven two. The exact setup that produced it.
Measure more than accuracy. Measure latency. Tokens per second. Memory used. Cost per hundred prompts. Failure rate. Format-following score. Refusal behaviour.
Because in real product work, the cheapest, fastest model that hits 90 percent of the quality often beats the prettiest leaderboard number that costs ten times more to serve.
Layer five. Runtime routing. A model ID is not enough. You need a routing decision.

The same model — say Qwen3 0.6B — can run on local CPU through llama dot cpp with a gguf file. Or on your laptop GPU through transformers. Or on a hosted vLLM endpoint. Or via a third-party inference provider.

Each path has different latency, different cost, different privacy properties, different cold-start behaviour, different supported features. Some support streaming. Some support tool calling. Some support batching.
The right question is not can this model run. The right question is — where should this model run for this user, this task, and this budget?
Cheapest path. Fastest path. Most private path. Highest quality path. Browser-only path. Demo path. These are different routes through the same artifact.
Most product teams pick one default and never revisit. The teams that do revisit — that maintain a routing layer in front of their models — ship faster, cheaper products.
Keep this layer in your head when you start building in Step 5.
Layer six. Agent-readable manifests. This is the layer most people haven't heard of yet, and the one that's going to matter most over the next two years.
Every model page on HuggingFace today is written for a human reading a screen. README files, prose paragraphs, screenshots. That's fine for humans.
But more and more of the actual model selection — for code completion, agent tools, RAG pipelines — is being done by AI agents calling APIs, not humans clicking links.

Agents need structured metadata. A JSON manifest that says: this model is for text generation, recommended runtimes are these, license is apache-2.0, safe to load is true, requires trust_remote_code is false, evals are these, related artifacts are these.
When you build something in this cohort, ask yourself — could an AI agent figure out how to use this without a human in the loop? If the answer is no, you've left work on the table.
Here's your homework. The Supply-Chain Audit.

Pick one model. I suggest Qwen slash Qwen3 0.6B because we're using it through the rest of the cohort. Write a short post on your Quarto blog answering eleven questions about it. The full list is on the lesson page below the player.
Most people just download. You're going to understand the system. Drop the post in the blog-share channel on Discord when it's up, and bring the audit to Friday's office hours.
See you in Step 2. Run a model locally.
